title: Experts liken sky-high fuel prices to three interest rate rises
text:
The economic shock from sky-high prices at the fuel pump is the equivalent of three interest rate rises, according to experts.

But despite fears that petrol and diesel shortages will worsen, the federal government says there are no plans for rationing yet.

Inflation dipped slightly to 3.7 per cent last month but that was before the war in the Middle East triggered a crisis at the pump.

READ MORE: Channel suddenly switches as thousands of ABC staff walk off the job

Experts are warning the economic shock from sky-high fuel prices is the equivalent of three interest rate rises. (9News)

"Inflation in Australia today, it's probably 5 per cent, possibly more," economist Chris Richardson told 9News

"That big increase in petrol prices is the equivalent by itself of something like three rate hikes."

That won't necessarily stop the Reserve Bank lifting rates again next month but Treasurer Jim Chalmers says it would be a consideration.

"Particularly for those Australians who can't substitute out of petrol or diesel, obviously that has a dampening effect," he said.

More than 600 service stations around Australia - or one in 12 - are without at least one type of fuel.

That won't necessarily stop the Reserve Bank lifting rates again next month but Treasurer Jim Chalmers says it would be a consideration. (9News)

Today, Energy Minister Chris Bowen offered some good news with alternative supply found for six cancelled oil tankers.

"In addition to replacing those six cancelled boats, they've been able to secure an additional three more cargo deliveries for April and May for Australia, over and above the normal contracted deliveries," Bowen said.

Pauline Hanson wants Bowen to declare a liquid fuel emergency that would give him the power to direct fuel to where its needed, while limiting fuel for motorists.

Petrol bowsers across NSW have begun running out of fuel. (Nine)

"We're calling for this emergency act to come into place that means there will have to be rationing," Hanson said.

Prime Minister Anthony Albanese disagreed.

"The states and territories can deliver fuel where it's needed most, and so the Commonwealth can concentrate on supply," he said.

An emergency response developed by the previous federal government suggested petrol rationing could be best done by imposing limits on all pumps - not that this is the minister's plan just yet.

By the minister's own admission, all ideas are being considered to boost fuel supply, including increasing ethanol content.

A non-descript office in the heart of Sydney's west into the NSW state government's war room in the battle for fuel. (9News)

But by late April if the Strait of Hormuz is not reopened, more drastic measures will be well and truly on the table.

To properly inform the community as to who has fuel - and who doesn't - 9News understands state and federal governments are planning a national website that records all retail outages.

NSW Premier Chris Minns today took extraordinary action to make sure the state was getting its fair share, turning a non-descript office in the heart of Sydney's west into the state government's war room in the battle for fuel.

Its officially called an emergency operation centre and has public servants tracking the oil companies' supply of fuel and then take control of oil distribution, directing it where it's needed most.

Australia Post have joined Uber, who were among the first to react to rising fuel prices. (9News)

"We have those extraordinary powers to re-direct resources, to inform the oil companies about where shortage are today where they're likely to be tomorrow," Minns said.

There's evidence today that some fuel is getting through. Yesterday, 51 service stations were completely dry in NSW, today that number dropped to 32.

But diesel is still running low with 187 servos empty, which is up from 164 yesterday.

There are warnings that the longer the shortages last, the more essential services will be impacted – rubbish collectors becoming the latest to raise the alarm.

"If we cant get the fuel supplies to collect for residences and business we have to make tough decision who gets collected and who don't," Brett Lemin from Waste Contractors and Recyclers Association said.

But by late April if the Strait of Hormuz is not reopened, more drastic measures will be well and truly on the table. (9News)

And then there's spiralling cost of fuel.

Australia Post today joined Uber, which was among the first to react to rising fuel prices.

Its contract customers who rely on StarTrack courier services will see the current fuel surcharge hiked from 4.8 per cent to 12 per cent.

Major retailers will be hit by this and it's expected they will pass it on to the customer.

title: Meta struck down in child negligence decision
text:
A﻿ landmark court decision has found Meta and YouTube liable for child harm, forcing the owner of Facebook and Instagram into a $US3 million ($4.31 million) payout.

A jury in California found the companies were "negligent in the design or operation of their platforms".

A woman, aged 20, brought the case against the tech giant, saying her use of social media as a child addicted her to the technology and "exacerbated her mental health struggles".

Mark Zuckerberg, chief executive officer of Meta Platforms Inc, exits Los Angeles Superior Court in February during the negligence case brought against his company. Photographer: Kyle Grillot/Bloomberg (Bloomberg)

A jury handed down the decision after more than 40 hours of deliberations across nine days. Along with the victim's payout, the jury also found the two companies negligent in the design or operation of their platforms.

Meta and YouTube were found to have "acted with malice, oppression or fraud", meaning the jury will hear further evidence to decide on punitive damages.

The victim, identified only as KGM or "Kaley", said she began using YouTube at the age of 6 and Instagram at the age of 9.

Parents hold images of children who have died after incidents of alleged bullying and blackmail occurred online, outside of the Los Angeles Superior Court. (Bloomberg)

She described being on social media "all day long", with her lawyers arguing they had features designed to "hook" young users, such as the "infinite" nature of feeds.﻿

Meta consistently argued that Kaley had struggled with her mental health separate from her social media use, often pointing to her turbulent home life.

Meta also said "not one of her therapists identified social media as the cause" of her mental health issues in a statement following closing arguments.

YouTube argued it was not a form of social media, but rather a video platform akin to television, and pointed to her declining use of the platform as she got older.

﻿It was determined that Meta and YouTube knew that the design of their platforms was dangerous or was likely to be dangerous when used by a minor. The jurors also said the platforms failed to adequately warn of the danger, which further contributed to the plaintiff's harm.

Meta and Google-owned YouTube were the two remaining defendants in the case after TikTok and Snap each settled before the trial began.

- Reported with Associated Press

title: 165-year-old rule stops woman retiring at home
text:
An Australian woman hoping to retire in her family's ancestral home of Italy has been blocked from obtaining citizenship after a 165-year-old rule was suddenly overturned.

Carla* considers herself to be Italian – she just needed the papers to prove it.

Her parents were both born in Italy, she speaks fluent Italian and is in nearly daily contact with her family members back in Europe.

But due to a new set of conditions imposed by the Italian government, Carla, who is aged in her 50s, may never become an Italian citizen.

A strict new rule has destroyed Carla's retirement plan to live in her parents' homeland of Italy. (Getty)

"I attended the Italian Consulate in Sydney in January this year to apply for Italian citizenship for myself and my son as both my parents were born in Italy and they were Italian when I was born in Australia," Carla told Nine.com.au.

"I was shocked and extremely disappointed to find out that, due to the Tajani Decree, I am not eligible for Italian citizenship any longer because my parents became Australian citizens while I was not yet 18.

"I feel like I have lost my identity after feeling Italian all my life and then be told in my 50s, I am no longer Italian."﻿

Carla wanted to take advantage of her blood right to citizenship to live in her mother's childhood home upon retirement.

Her son was hoping to do the same.﻿

Their plight for citizenship has been derailed by a controversial 2025 emergency decree, known as the Tajani decree, which seeks to ﻿tighten eligibility criteria for Italian citizenship.﻿

Do you have a story? Contact reporter April Glover at april.glover@nine.com.au

The Italian Embassy in Canberra. (The Italian Embassy in Canberra)

Under the old "jure sanguinis"﻿ (right of blood) rule, anyone who could prove they had an Italian ancestor alive after March 17, 1861 – when the Kingdom of Italy was founded – could obtain citizenship.

But now, the decree, which has been upheld in Italy's constitutional court, has ruled that only those with a parent or grandparent born in Italy can obtain an Italian passport.﻿

The legislation also denies citizenship to those with an Italian parent or grandparent who held dual citizenship at the time of their birth.

Because Carla's parents became Australian citizens before she reached adulthood, she has been stripped of her right to an Italian passport.﻿

"My husband is eligible for Italian citizenship even though his parents were born in Australia because his grandfather did not become an Australian citizen until my mother-in-law was over 18," Carla added.

"My husband does not speak Italian or have any contact with relatives in Italy."

Italy's Minister of Foreign Affairs Antonio Tajani said he introduced the decree to lower the risk of "passport shoppers".

"Being an Italian citizen is a serious thing," Tajani said last year.

"Unfortunately, over the years, there have been abuses and citizenship requests that went somewhat beyond genuine interest in our country."

The Italian passport was ranked as the third most powerful in the world, according to the Henley Passport Index.

Italy's Minister of Foreign Affairs Antonio Tajani. (AP/Virginia Mayo)

Carla said this rule will now deny an entire diaspora of Italians the chance to live and work in their family's homeland.

"We are extremely lucky to live in this wonderful country of Australia that has been enriched by the multitude of varied migrants that have flourished on these shores," she said.

"I am not optimistic the diaspora of Italian heritage people like me will be recognised.

"As my lovely son-in-law recently said to me, 'you don't need a piece of paper to say you are Italian, because you definitely are Italian''."

Nine.com.au has contacted ﻿the Consulate of Italy and Embassy of Italy in Canberra for comment.

*Not her real name.﻿

title: Travelling family left blindsided by $500 tank of fuel
text:
A﻿ family of four who sold everything they owned to travel Australia in a caravan have been left blindsided by the soaring cost of diesel.

Sydney couple Lisa and Blake Walsh ﻿put their house on the market last year and took their two children and dog on an adventure of a lifetime.

The past seven months of travelling the country have been bliss – but it has almost become too expensive to keep driving due to the astronomical prices at the pump.

Lisa and Blake Walsh ﻿put their house on the market and began travelling Australia with their two kids and dog. (Instagram/@gone_walshabout)

The family paid nearly $500 to fill up at a petrol station in Rosebery on Tasmania's west coast.

"We have two kids and a dog, we've got our whole lives with us, so we are very heavy and fuel was already our biggest expense," Lisa told Nine.com.au.

"And now that that's completely blown out of the water, our whole budget's shot.

"We've really got to go completely back to the drawing board."

Diesel at a Tas Petroleum station in Rosebery was priced at $3.09.9 per litre yesterday.

This is slightly above the average of $3 per litre for diesel in Tasmania, according to FuelCheck.

The couple didn't even fill up an entire tank and it still set them back a staggering $495.

The family paid nearly $500 to fill up at a petrol station in Rosebery on Tasmania's west coast. (Instagram/@gone_walshabout)

Diesel at a Tas Petroleum station in Rosebery was priced at $3.09.9 per litre yesterday. (Instagram/@gone_walshabout)

"We can't afford to pay much more than this. Realistically, we're going to drain our savings pretty quickly," Lisa added.

"We will just [have to] pull up somewhere and get some work."

Lisa and her family are aiming to stay on the road for another two years, using money from the sale of their house to keep afloat, provided that fuel doesn't deplete their savings too quickly.

Other travellers have already buckled under the financial pressure.

Caravan parks are under threat of sitting empty as regional operators worry travellers will simply stay home over the Easter break .

"Some people that we know have just said that they're going to stop and work," Lisa said.﻿

"I've seen cancellations left, right and centre online.

"People posting on Facebook groups saying, 'Oh hey, I had this site booked over Easter, but we're not going anymore'."

Lisa and her family are aiming to stay on the road for another two years. (Instagram/@gone_walshabout)

Sydney travellers Brad and Karen, who take their caravan out on holidays and over weekends, told Nine.com.au that their personal limit is $4 per litre for diesel.

Despite growing nationwide concerns that fuel could nudge this price, the couple desperately hope it won't come to that.﻿

"We haven't cancelled trips yet, but now budgeting $1000 in fuel or $4/l – effectively doubling the budget to cover costs for our next short trip, then assess the next big trip on what happens with fuel costs," Karen explained.

"Our last tank was $120 for only 43 litres of diesel and 280km travelled."

title: Truckie left stranded twice on remote highways as diesel runs dry
text:
Truck drivers are facing dangerous situations as diesel shortages in Australia's remote outback leave them stranded on isolated stretches of highway.

Long-distance driver Robert Cook has been stuck twice on his Melbourne to Perth run, first for over 24 hours on the Nullarbor Plain and again in Keith in South Australia's south-east.

Cook waited hours alongside five other drivers for a fresh supply of diesel to be delivered to a remote fuel station on SA's Limestone Coast yesterday evening.

Long-distance driver Robert Cook has been stuck twice on his Melbourne to Perth run. (Supplied)

He had been driving for around 500 kilometres without seeing a single drop of diesel at any service station.﻿

"﻿Some of the guys I've been speaking to have said, 'Look, we're thinking about hanging our keys up'," Cook, who runs his own heavy haulage company Helco Group, told Nine.com.au.

"I've had to stand down two trucks. It's a struggle."

After filling up late yesterday, Cook said he may only manage to make it to Brisbane before needing to fork out for another tank, if he drives "conservatively".

Diesel fuel costs for Cook's regular Melbourne to Perth drive have now doubled from $5000 to $10,000.

The cost ﻿of diesel is now over $3.15 per litre at some petrol stations.

A line of trucks waiting for a fresh delivery of diesel in SA. (Supplied)

Although the ballooning price of keeping his trucks on the﻿ road is not the biggest problem.

Careful fuel management is crucial even without a global oil crisis – at one point, there is a 192-kilometre gap between fuel stops on the Nullarbor, which is among the longest straight stretches of road in the country.

Cook is now worried for the safety of his drivers.﻿

"I tell our drivers, minimum 20 to 30 litres of water, enough canned food and stuff... because our trucks have microwaves and inverters, so they're completely self-reliant," he said.

"It can go south very quickly, especially out there in the summertime.

"It's very dangerous."

Cook is on the road for a job with freight carrier Loadshift.

Loadshift's Operations Coordinator Alex Randall said drivers like Cook are being left "high and dry", despite trucking companies being the backbone of Australia's supply chain.

"There's no system telling them which servos have fuel and which don't. They're driving blind," Randall said.

"What makes this even worse is that a third of trucks on Australian roads are running empty at any given time. "﻿

Fuel costs for Cook's regular Melbourne to Perth run have now doubled from $5000 to $10,000. (Supplied)

The Transport Workers Union (TWU) and Australian Road Transport Industrial Organisation (ARTIO) have also warned surging diesel prices have now reached "deadly" new heights.

The unions have called on the government to pass emergency laws to keep truck drivers in business.﻿

"Businesses are at imminent risk of collapse and workers are under deadly pressure because there are huge retailers and other clients out there refusing to pay their fair share for skyrocketing fuel costs," TWU National Secretary Michael Kaine said.

"The federal government must act now to put in place emergency powers to ensure the industry can cope with the current crisis – otherwise we will see more transport workers die on our roads, more businesses go under, and our supply chains at breaking point."

The time spent waiting for diesel to arrive has put Cook almost two days' behind schedule.

He warned the knock-on impact is not "sustainable" and that prices will inevitably rise across the board.

"T﻿he problem is that we have to pass that on to somebody, and unfortunately, it's going to be the consumer," he said.

"It's double for us, it's going to be double for them, just to stay afloat. It's going to affect a lot of people, like, without a doubt."

title: Tradies hit hardest by fuel crisis forced to raise prices or go bust
text:
Exclusive: The fuel crisis is hitting tradies harder than most and the rest of Australia could soon feel the sting as businesses are forced to raise prices or go bust.

Jac Northam, 26, recently started his joinery company Jac Design and used to spend about $150 a week on fuel.

Now it's nearly double that at $285 a week – and the cost just keeps rising.

Tradesman Jac Northam, 26, used to spend $150 a week on fuel. Now it's almost double. (Supplied)

It could have dire consequences for the young tradesman.

"I've had to reduce what I pay myself and avoid tolls as much as possible to make sure I've got extra money in my business to cover it," he told nine.com.au.

"This is going to have a major knock-on effect not just in my life, but within the industry."

Have you got a story? Contact reporter Maddison Leach at mleach@nine.com.au

The fuel crisis is putting huge financial strain on tradespeople, especially those who run small businesses and don't have a guaranteed income every week.

Many are already ﻿contending with ﻿surging material costs due to supply chain disruptions caused by the war in the Middle East.

Now petrol has surpassed $2.50 a litre in many locations and diesel, which many trade work vehicles run on, is over $3 a litre in parts of Australia.

Hundreds of servos across the country are currently without at least one type of fuel .

The government has suggested Aussies work from home to save cash and conserve fuel but that's just not an option for tradespeople.

"How do you expect a whole industry that relies on cars and fuel to provide income to stay at home?" Northam said.

"I'd love to do my part and save fuel for the people that need it most, like the farmers and emergency services, but how am I meant to get a van full of tools to site everyday?"

He suspects the situation get worse before it gets better.

That's bad news for small businesses that don't have the money to absorb rising fuel prices for much longer.

﻿Nikki Chamberlain fears she may have to pass increased costs onto her customers. (Supplied)

﻿Nikki Chamberlain runs Specialized Garage Door , a garage door maintenance and installation business in Perth, and her staff drive between up to eight sites every day.

The business' fuel bill has exploded by more than 20 per cent in the last few weeks.﻿

READ MORE: MAFS psychologist Mel Schilling dies aged 54

"It's so much money and ﻿there's nothing that we can do to reduce it, we don't have the option of working from home," Chamberlain told nine.com.au.

"It's very scary at the moment for small business owners."﻿

The small family business is absorbing the cost for the time being with the hopes it will recoup the cash once the crisis has eased.

Chamberlain doesn't want to push the added cost onto clients because it could hurt the business in the long-term.

But if prices don't drop soon, she may have no other choice.﻿

﻿"We won't be the only business that does, everyone will just have to increase their cost of business or their cost of sale," Chamberlain said.

"Then it will become a case of either people will go elsewhere if they can find cheaper options, or they decide to hold off certain services."

Sale Painting and Decorating owner Stuart Sale confessed he'll likely have to start adjust quotes if fuel costs don't let up.

His full bill has almost doubled in the last few weeks and though he's "cracking on" for now, the business won't stay profitable if he doesn't start charging clients more.

"I do worry," he told nine.com.au.

"If I were to increase my rates it would impact me considerably, because it's tough to win jobs without competitively pricing."

Sale Painting and Decorating owner Stuart Sale is worried about staying competitive in the fuel crisis. (Supplied)

It's tough to stay competitive on cost at the best of times and he'd prefer not to pass on extra costs to his customers.

But if things don't let up, he may not have a choice.﻿

﻿Businesses that don't or can't find ways around skyrocketing fuel prices could go bust unless the federal or state governments step in.

Some tradespeople have called for a subsidy or tax break to support the industries hit the hardest by the fuel crisis.﻿

Carpenter and site supervisor Rodrigo Zanchetta, 39, is among them.

"A government subsidy would definitely help people that can't work from home and financially support their families," he told nine.com.au.

He used to spend about $200 on fuel each week, now it's setting him back $300.

Carpenter Rodrigo Zanchetta said government support would make a huge difference to tradespeople. (Supplied)

﻿Zanchetta has been forced to dip into his savings to cover it and he's stressed about the long-term financial impacts if the fuel crisis worsens.

Without a subsidy or some other financial support﻿, he fears tradespeople without stable finances won't be able to make ends meet.

"As little as it sounds, a couple hundred bucks goes a long way﻿."

title: Sydney Opera House sails light up to mark 50 Years of Apple
text:
The sails of the Sydney Opera House will light up with colour over the next three nights as part of a global celebration of Apple's 50th birthday .

A brightly coloured celebration of creativity will kick off a year of collaboration between Apple and the Sydney Opera House with newly commissioned artworks labelled Illuminating Creativity, which is a showcase of original artwork created on iPad using the Australian app Procreate.

Earlier this month, members of the public were also encouraged to create their own digital art at Apple stores across Australia, with those artworks submitted to be part of this week's projection on the Eastern sails.

The artwork It's Beautiful Down Here by Rebecca Mills. (Supplied)

Opera House CEO Louise Herron AM said: "The Sydney Opera House, like Apple, is synonymous with creativity. This collaboration is a natural fit between two organisations that continue to search for new ways for people to experience the world. From our first international children's festival to exciting programming highlights throughout the year, Apple's support will enable us to continue to inspire and welcome more of the community in line with our ambition to be Everyone's House."

Apple's senior vice president of Worldwide Marketing, Greg Joswiak, said: "For 50 years, Apple has been at the forefront of empowering creativity, providing tools that allow people to imagine, design, and share their unique visions with the world. The yearlong collaboration with the Sydney Opera House and Illuminating Creativity is a celebration of this legacy, bringing together the incredible talent of Australian artists and the transformative power of iPad and Procreate. We are thrilled to be working with such an iconic Australian cultural landmark to help inspire the next generation of creatives."

Ryhia Dank AKA nardurna's Memory sails. (Supplied)

Sydney's Opera House lighting up with iPad artworks is part of a global celebration of Apple's 50th birthday, with events taking place across the world in places like China, Bangkok, Seoul, and New York City, where Alicia Keys performed at Grand Central Station.

Some of the artists being highlighted in lights include Aussies Jonathan Zawada from Northern NSW with artwork inspired by the ground beneath your feet, and a stunning underwater-inspired creation "It's Beautiful Down Here" by Perth-based Rebecca Mills.

Kentaro Yoshida's A FRAME (Supplied)

There's a stunning First Nations artwork by Ryhia Dank, a Gudanji, Wakaja, and Kalkadoon woman whose artwork shows the journey of a goanna moving through the bush.

It's worth getting a harbour-side seat to take in all the art, including a spectacular creation which captures the Aussie surf and sun - tilted "A FRAME" with its wave shape plans with the form of the Opera House Sails and was created by Sydney-based Japanese artist Kentaro Yoshida.

There's even a glimpse of Aussie suburban life, with Phoebe Paradise's "A Brisbane Story" capturing a little balcony in Brisbane to show the sheer "average-ness" of the night sky and a porch light - it's well worth a look.

All these artworks and more will be displayed over the next three nights.

title: Lift minimum wage above the rate of inflation, federal government urges
text:
Low-paid workers in the hospitality, retail and healthcare sectors should not "bear the brunt" of cost-of-living pressures and must receive a pay rise above inflation, the federal government has told the workplace umpire.

In their submission to the Fair Work Commission's annual review of the minimum wage, Employment Minister Amanda Rishworth and Treasurer Jim Chalmers have asked for a "real" increase to the fortnightly rate of $948 per full-time fortnight.

Similar submissions have been made every year under the Albanese government, which has urged that the 2.7 million workers who earn either the minimum rate or the various award wage rates tied to the minimum "do not go backwards".

"These are our hardworking workers that are some of our lowest-paid. They deserve a wage increase," Ms Rishworth said.

Each year, the Fair Work Commission takes submissions from government, business groups and unions before making its own determination. Peak union the ACTU has sought a 5 per cent hike, and business group ACCI has asked for 3.5 per cent.

Government non-committal on the magic number

Consistent with its usual approach, the government has not sought a specific number and has been ambiguous about what would constitute a "real" increase.

While that term generally means above the rate of inflation, the wage determinations apply from July 1 for the next financial year, meaning it is not possible to use today's backward-looking inflation figures to say whether an increase is real or not.

Typically, the Fair Work Commission makes observations about real changes over several years. In its 2024 determination, it observed that minimum-wage workers had gone backwards since 2021 but that inflation was still a worry, settling on 3.75 per cent.

In its 2025 determination, it relied on the Reserve Bank's confidence that inflation was going to head back under 3 per cent, declaring that "this inflationary episode is now over" and choosing 3.5 per cent in the hope that this would prove a real wage hike.

The uptick in inflation since then has dashed those hopes. The latest inflation figures for February showed a rate of 3.7 per cent or 3.3 per cent, using either the headline figure or the RBA's preferred "underlying" figure.

Jim Chalmers has warned inflation predictions from Treasury of as much as 5 per cent could be conservative. (ABC News: Luke Stephenson)

But it is almost certain to surge by July given the inflationary pressure emanating from the Middle East war, with Mr Chalmers warning this week that even Treasury projections of headline inflation nearing 5 per cent may be "conservative".

The risk of higher inflation both underlines the government's case that low-paid workers will be under financial strain, but also adds to the risk posed by any wage rise.

Ms Rishworth refused to engage in hypotheticals about the appropriate figure on Thursday, saying only that the Fair Work Commission considered "a lot of numbers" and acknowledging that there was an unusual amount of volatility this year.

She emphasised that, although they represent one in five of all workers, minimum and award wage-earners account for just 13 per cent of the wage bill. They are also more likely to be women and more likely to be in casual jobs.

The government's submissions suggest any real increase should be "economically sustainable" and consistent with inflation returning to the Reserve Bank's target range.

"We believe that there is a pathway for … a real wage increase that does not put pressure on inflation," she said.

title: Ryswyk says reaction to coming out has been 'overwhelming'
text:
Former Brisbane Lions player Leigh Ryswyk says the support he has received after becoming the first AFL player to come out as gay has "blown me away".

Ryswyk publicly came out as gay yesterday during an interview on Melbourne FM station Joy 94.9, but said his friends had known for several years.

The 41-year-old spoke on ABC 891 Adelaide on Thursday morning and said the reaction he received since coming out had been overwhelming.

Former Brisbane Lion becomes first openly gay AFL player Former Brisbane Lions player Leigh Ryswyk has become the first openly gay AFL player, past or present.

"[It] has been amazing to be honest, overwhelming support, love, messages from people from everywhere. I can't lie, it's blown me away," he said.

Ryswyk played one AFL match for the Brisbane Lions in 2005, followed by a successful career in the SANFL where he played more than 200 matches for North Adelaide.

Asked if he believed he could have come out while still in the AFL, Ryswyk said he was not ready to make that public at the time.

"Different things happen within your life and you just move forward," he said.

"When I was ready, I was ready and that's now."

Ryswyk said he hoped his story could help others sportsmen feel comfortable to "be themselves" and that there would come a time in society when announcing a person's sexuality would not be necessary.

ABC Sport Daily podcast ABC Sport Daily is your daily sports conversation. We dive into the biggest story of the day and get you up to speed with everything else that's making headlines.

He said while AFL clubs and the AFL community would largely be supportive of queer players, crowd behaviour could be a factor why many did not feel comfortable being openly gay.

"The club itself will just wrap their arms around the player, the AFL community as a whole will do that — the outside world, fans and media at times … then maybe that fear [from] them," he said.

"It doesn't matter what happens on the footy field, if someone misses a football kick and they can get trolled, right?

"People are very passionate about their footy teams and want to see them succeed, but the environment needs to change and education continue to happen and that's why we need to start at a younger level because again it's learnt through that level, once everyone's on board, we want to feel safe anywhere we go."

Brisbane Lions chief executive Sam Graham released a statement on Thursday morning, expressing his hope that Ryswyk's story could make the sport more inclusive.

"It is great to see Leigh share his story and journey publicly," Graham said.

"We want our game to be an inclusive environment for everyone, including players, staff, members and fans.

Mitch Brown on the hate that's come with coming out After coming out, Mitch Brown became an unofficial spokesperson for queer issues in the AFL.

"As a club, we hope this has a positive impact across football and society more broadly."

North Adelaide also released a statement, throwing its support behind Ryswyk, who is a life member at the club.

"The North Adelaide Football Club supports former player Leigh Ryswyk, who has come out publicly as the first openly gay past or present AFL/SANFL player," the club said in a statement.

"Leigh played 226 games for the club, is a North Adelaide Football Club Life Member and SANFL Life Member, and was a key contributor to our 2018 Reserves Premiership, overcoming broken ribs suffered late in the home and away season.

"As a football club, we pride ourselves on fostering an inclusive environment where players, staff, members and supporters from all communities feel safe, respected, and free to be themselves."

title: Australian bishop appointed by pope to senior Vatican role
text:
A New South Wales bishop has been appointed by Pope Leo XIV to a senior role in the Vatican.

Anthony Randazzo, the bishop of Broken Bay, which since 2019 covers Sydney's northern regions and the central coast, has received the personal title of archbishop and been named as Prefect of the Dicastery for Legislative Texts.

The position in the Roman Curia — the administrative wing of the Catholic Church — is responsible for interpreting church laws and ensuring their correct application around the world.

The appointment makes him the most senior Australian at the Vatican since the late cardinal George Pell, who was prefect of the secretariat for the economy at the Vatican from 2014 to 2019.

Archbishop Randazzo at St Patrick's Catholic Church in East Gosford. (Supplied: Facebook)

In a statement on social media, Archbishop Randazzo said he was "profoundly grateful" to Pope Leo for the trust placed in him and asked for prayers as he undertakes the new challenge.

The 59-year-old met with Leo in the Apostolic Palace, his official residence in Vatican City, earlier this month.

Archbishop Randazzo was born in Sydney and raised in Queensland before being ordained as a priest in the Archdiocese of Brisbane in 1991.

His academic pathway as a priest took him to Rome, where he earned qualifications in canon law from the Pontifical Gregorian University.

The newly assigned archbishop is due to take up the role in Rome in the next few months. (Supplied: Facebook)

He subsequently spent five years working at the Congregation for the Doctrine of the Faith.

Archbishop Randazzo is also the president of the Federation of Catholic Bishops' Conferences of Oceania.

He will remain in Broken Bay for the next three months before taking on his new role in Rome.

The pope is due to visit Australia in 2028, the first papal visit since Pope Benedict XVI in 2008.

title: Live: Australia, Germany to sign status of forces agreement
text:
Yesterday, Energy Minister Chris Bowen confirmed the six ships carrying oil bound for Australia that had been cancelled had been replaced, and he'd also secured three additional ships.

Shadow Defence Minister James Paterson remains concerned.

"There's a big lag time between the ships that are already on the water on their way to Australia and the crude which is ultimately extracted in the Middle East and then refined in Asia before it's sent to us," Paterson told Sky. "It will take time before that failure to get crude out of the Strait of Hormuz into Asia is actually felt in the refineries and that's when the problem will come."

Paterson also notes the energy minister has "extraordinary" powers under the Liquid Fuel Security Act that he could be using to lean on companies to get supply to where it's needed.

"It gives them visibility of the supply and demand issues across the economy, and great authority to lean on energy companies, fuel companies, distributors, wholesalers, intermediaries to make sure that supply gets to where it needs to go. So, Chris Bowen is not rolling up his sleeves and getting on to that task."

He doesn't quite go as far as calling on the government to trigger the Act.

Again, Bowen has always maintained the bar to pull the emergency trigger under that Act is extremely high.

title: TGA releases proposed reforms to Australian sunscreen regulation
text:
The Therapeutic Goods Administration (TGA) has proposed sweeping reforms to Australian sunscreen regulations.

The proposals include changes to SPF testing requirements, oversight of the labs that do SPF testing, and the way SPF is labelled — reforms that would overhaul the sunscreen industry.

One of the options flagged is removing SPF numbers and replacing them with low, medium, high and very high ratings.

The TGA has opened the draft proposals for public consultation.

It said the proposed reforms came after monitoring international sunscreen developments, as well as developments in Australia, including SPF testing by consumer group CHOICE and media reports by the ABC.

CHOICE last year revealed 16 out of 20 popular sunscreens didn't meet their SPF label claims.

Failures at Australian sunscreen maker revealed The Australian sunscreen manufacturer at the centre of a spate of recent product recalls has been blocked from exporting sunscreens to the United States for the past three years.

An ABC investigation also revealed preliminary SPF tests had called into doubt the accuracy of SPF ratings of more sunscreens that hadn't been tested by Choice, as well as the way they were being sold.

The TGA referenced this reporting in its consultation paper on sunscreen regulation reform today.

"An ABC investigation revealed that a number of different products marketed in Australia were being supplied under the one Australian Register of Therapeutic Goods identification number, as well as raising concerns relating to a number of sunscreen products not meeting their claimed SPF rating," the TGA said.

"These developments, alongside our own internal reviews and investigations, have prompted the TGA to review aspects of the current regulatory framework for sunscreens."

The TGA said it was seeking to improve the regulation of sunscreens in Australia to ensure consumers had confidence in the protection offered.

"Given Australia has the highest rates of skin cancer and melanoma in the world, with around 2,000 people dying each year, it is critical that the regulatory settings are appropriate to ensure consumer confidence in sunscreens," the regulator said.

The proposals focus on improving the reliability of SPF testing, strengthening oversight of testing laboratories and simplifying SPF labelling.

These seven sunscreens were among those tested by CHOICE that came up lower than their SPF50+ claims. (ABC News: Billy Cooper)

Consumer group CHOICE welcomed the announcement after it raised the alarm on the test results last year.

"Proposals to improve and expand testing requirements, require accreditation for testing laboratories, and increase transparency will help restore consumer trust in the reliability of SPF claims," Director of Campaigns Andy Kelly said.

" Without [CHOICE's] investigation, it's likely these products would still be available for sale – a clear example of why we urgently need stronger regulation in the sunscreen sector. "

However, the group is against one of the TGA's suggestions, to reform the SPF rating system — introduced in the 1960s — with words or graphics.

Examples of the graphics the TGA has proposed to improve the SPF rating system. (Supplied: TGA)

The regulator said there were "misconceptions" about the SPF rating scale and what it meant in real terms, suggesting a scale from "low" to "very high" could make it easier for consumers to quickly interpret protection levels.

"The SPF scale is not linear. The difference in the amount of sun protection between an SPF of 30 and 50 is minimal, and this difference becomes even smaller as the SPF value increases," the TGA said.

"Clear categories help consumers select products based on their needs (e.g. everyday use vs high UV exposure)."

However, it acknowledged changing labelling requirements would need legislative amendments and industry investment and would create divergence with other countries that use SPF numerical ratings.

Making test results public considered by TGA

Currently, sunscreen makers are not required to provide their SPF testing data to the TGA, which means there is limited transparency around the evidence underpinning an SPF rating.

The TGA has proposed a change that would require that information to be made public so it can be subject to appropriate scrutiny by researchers and consumer groups.

Questions over lab's sunscreen tests At least eight sunscreens that failed to meet their advertised SPF50 sun protection claims in a recent review had their original tests for certification done by an overseas laboratory whose results are being questioned by industry experts.

"Open data supports modern regulatory trends toward transparency and evidence-based decision making," it said.

The regulator said there could be drawbacks to this approach, however, particularly around the exposure of trade secrets relating to formulation details.

"Publicly available data could be used by competitors to reverse-engineer formulations or benchmark products unfairly," the TGA said.

title: Here's who you should see at comedy festivals across Australia
text:
Luke Heggie was 37 when he did his first show at Melbourne International Comedy Festival in 2012.

"I showed up every year after that, and within a decade or so, I was able to send enough money home to pay the rent," he says.

Two years after that first festival, he was performing a sold-out show in a 50-seat venue.

"A bloke was on his own in the front row, maggot," he says. "He began snoring. I wasn't sure everyone could hear him, so I picked up the stand and put the mic right in his face.

"He didn't snore when I wanted him to, though, so I poked him in the nose with the microphone to wake him up."

Now, 13 shows later, with a reputation as one of the most hard-working comics in the business, gigging most nights of the week, Heggie is returning to Melbourne comedy festival to a hopefully more wakeful audience.

He's armed with a compilation of jokes from the last five years titled I Won't Say It Again.

"A lot of people don't forgive hearing the same joke twice," he says. "These are the same people who go to legacy bands and demand to hear a song that made them happy before their lives turned bad.

"I've forgotten a lot of the material, so there's no way someone else has remembered it all."

As comedy festival season continues, with Melbourne now in full swing, we ask Heggie and some of Australia's other favourite comedians to tell us which up-and-coming acts to catch this year.

Luke Heggie

I don't know if "up-and-coming" is an insult, as I still get called that from time to time, but some comics I like who should be better known are Rohan Arneil, Billy D'Arcy, Alexandra Hudson and Kate Dolan. All Sydney. All good. Do it.

Luke Heggie: I Won't Say It Again is at Melbourne, Sydney, Perth and Brisbane comedy festivals.

Aaron Chen

Aaron Chen filmed his first Netflix special in New York last year. (Supplied: Netflix)

I'd always go out of my way to see Dan Rath. Lara Ricote is going, she's so funny. Mark Silcox is going, which would be such a treat to see. And I love Sam Campbell and Danielle Walker. So many people, I would struggle to name them all!

And Daniel Connell and Kate Dolan — I have truly giggled from them A LOT.

Aaron Chen: Funny Garden is streaming on Netflix from March 31.

Hannah Gadsby

Hannah Gadsby, who recently released an album of her latest live show, Woof!, hosts a kind of talk show at MICF. (Supplied: Token/Mia Mala McDonald)

I can't recommend Abby Wambaugh's The First 3 Minutes of 17 Shows enough. Abby is a brilliant, rare talent and this show is so many good things. It's genius, it's refreshing, it is so, so funny. And you have to be in the room to experience the real magic. If you're going to take a punt on any show this festival, this is the one. It has been the absolute talk of the town in Edinburgh the last two years, and sold out shows in NYC, LA, and London, so I'm really honoured to be presenting it at MICF this year in association with the comedy festival.

Also, I'd be remiss if I didn't suggest Birds by Alex Hines and Sarah Stafford. The sheer force of imagination between those two is incomprehensible. This show is going to be batshit in the best possible way and I can't wait to see it.

Hannah Gadsby: The Evening Muse is at Melbourne International Comedy Festival.

Rove McManus

McManus hosted Rove Live for 11 seasons, as well as the short-lived Saturday Night Rove, and will speak about his talk-show experiences in his comedy festival hour. (Supplied: Token)

Bridie Connell has created one of the greatest shows I have witnessed this festival run with Fuccbois. It's a boy band played completely by women and the premise is you are witnessing the last-ever concert of their final-ever world tour. The songs are original, hilarious and catchy as hell. That's all you need to know. Thank me later.

I had the absolute pleasure of working with Abby Howells on something late last year that we're not allowed to talk about yet and she is sweet and delightful and heartbreakingly endearing, but more importantly she is sharp and funny and purely original. I can't wait to get to see her in full flight for an entire show.

Why Anisa Nandaula swapped a six-figure day job for the stand-up stage After years of doing three gigs a night, Ugandan-born, Brisbane-based comedian Anisa Nandaula was finally able to follow her passion right to the Melbourne International Comedy Festival.

My queen Anisa Nandaula is so bloody good at what she does you can be fooled into thinking she has been at this for a lot longer than she has. Her debut solo show last year brought the house down and she has followed it up again this year with No Small Talk to truly cement her legacy as a comic that will end up being one of the greatest this country has ever been fortunate enough to lay claim to.

Alexei Toliopoulos takes his first steps as a solo performer in VHS and it is a beautiful piece that is the perfect way to introduce yourself to someone who is beloved by all of us in the comedy industry.

I know Tom Ballard is by no means an up-and-comer but his new show Be Funny Challenge (Impossible) is a goddamn masterpiece. I saw it in Perth when it was still in its infancy, so can only imagine how much more impressive it is now (if that's even possible).

Alex Ward has written a really lovely show with laughs and heart called Literally Anyone. I adore Wardy and you should take all your friends to go see her because it will make you seem really cool when she becomes everyone's new favourite comic.

And don't bother reading about how Bron Lewis has found her sweet spot on stage and is killing it right now. She's already sold out. You took too long.

Tonight's Guest: Rove McManus (Tales from the Talk Show Trenches) is at Melbourne, Sydney and Brisbane comedy festivals.

Anisa Nandaula

Anisa Nandaula represents Gen Z on the new iteration of Talkin' 'Bout Your Gen, hosted by fellow comic Anne Edmonds. (Supplied: Jubilee Street/Nick Robertson)

I'm looking forward to seeing Bron Lewis, Ray O'Leary and Melanie Bracewell and I'm very excited to see Chris Parker. And in terms of an up-and-comer, comedian Rachael Hornbuckle is very funny, so I'm excited to see her hour.

Anisa Nandaula: No Small Talk is at Melbourne, Sydney and Brisbane comedy festivals, before touring Cairns, Gold Coast, Sunshine Coast, Wollongong, Geelong, Toowoomba, Darwin and Newcastle.

Dave Thornton

Dave Thornton is a comedian, actor and broadcaster, known for Upper Middle Bogan, hosting brekkie radio in Melbourne, and Sam Pang Tonight. (Supplied: Token/Ian Laidlaw)

Nikki Britton makes me laugh so hard and it's just a joy seeing her shows. She's based in Sydney so we don't get to see her perform that much in Melbourne, which means it's a real treat when she's down here doing a new show. A very funny comic.

Hannah Camilleri puts together these one-person sketch shows that blow my mind. She weaves sketches in and out of the hour, performs amazing characters and even interacts with the audience (don't worry, you'll be safe, you're a partner in the show, not the butt of her jokes). She's won heaps of awards, so obviously she's a top-shelf performer!

Dave Thornton: More or Less is at Melbourne, Sydney, Perth, Brisbane and Newcastle comedy festivals.

Emma Holland

Emma Holland is known for her appearances on Have You Been Paying Attention? and Taskmaster Australia. (Supplied: Jubilee Street)

Ben Hunter: He's a very strange boy. He's like if one of the orphans from Oliver had parents and a loving support network. I've been a fan of Ben since I first saw him in 2022, he's got a really unique voice and I'm often inspired by his ability to juice a punchline for all it's worth. I'll watch anything/everything he does.

Meg Jäger: She's a very strange girl. She's like if the burn book from Mean Girls came to life but had social anxiety (non-derogatory). Meg could make me laugh just from the tone she says things in. Meg is someone who is fully in control of her persona and has the punchlines to match. She's a weapon.

Kate Dolan: She's a very strange woman. She's like if Ms Trunchbull had a soft spot for karaoke and Wind in the Willows. Kate is a powerhouse performer. She bursts onto stage and doesn't pick her foot up from the pedal for the whole show. She's also an incredibly intelligent writer and I'm very in awe of her creativity. I love Kate.

Hot Department: They're very strange siblings/couple (I'm not sure what their dynamic is). They're like if RuPaul's fracking habits were a physical being. I've never seen two people commit to bits more. I laugh from my entire gut every time I watch them. I think I saw their 2024 show seven times in one year. They're my favourite comedians.

Emma Holland: The Dog Dies at the Start is at Melbourne, Sydney, Perth and Brisbane comedy festivals, before touring Wollongong, Gold Coast and Sunshine Coast.

Emma Holland: Judge Emma is at Melbourne International Comedy Festival.

Alex Ward

Alex Ward is a regular on panel shows including Have You Been Paying Attention? and ABC TV's Guy Montgomery's Guy Mont Spelling Bee. (Supplied: Token/Ian Laidlaw)

This question makes me sweat. I think, oh God, why didn't I watch more of the other acts on the line-up? Why did I spend half the night on my phone in the green room?

Stupid, stupid phone.

I'm not meaning to be rude or disinterested. I just have to prep for my spot … and I can't do that until I finish the Sudoku I started on the tram. But now I'm thinking about it … and I'm already on my phone! I'll just quickly re-check the acts on Instagram.

OK, got some recs. Thank you, beautiful, beautiful phone.

The newish funny people you should go see: Lena Moon, Carmelo Costa, Mish Wittrup, Con Coutis and Ben Hunter.

Alex Ward: Literally Anyone is at Melbourne, Sydney and Brisbane comedy festivals.

David Correos

David Correos starred in Taskmaster NZ in 2021, against Urzila Carlson and Guy Montgomery. (Supplied: Century)

Sean Collier is a very exciting up-and-coming comic from New Zealand. Some of my favourite political takes from someone who looks like they're still cooked from the night before. I've been loving seeing what Sean's been doing lately and he's saying stuff that I wish I could get away with.

It's so sick that Melbourne brought Christopher Macarthur-Boyd over. I'm such a big fan of this guy, you get to see him with a tight 20 this year. Incredible craftsman when it comes to comedy. Jealous of how precise yet effortless his stand-up is. One of the best joke writers I've met. I'm excited to watch CMB perform this year!

You gotta see Angella Dravid's show this year! She won the biggest comedy award in New Zealand last year. No matter what direction Angella takes her show each night, it's gonna be such a fun ride. I know her show has been meticulously refined this year but also here's hoping you go on a night where she just says what's on her mind. Angella's got one of my favourite brains.

Helen Bauer makes me scream-laugh. You know how sometimes when you're playing mates on Smash Bros, they start combo-ing you to the point where you can't even input moves so you're just kinda helpless and stunned? Helen does that with laughs.

David Correos: Touching My Active Mind is at Melbourne, Sydney and Brisbane comedy festivals.

Gillian Cosgriff

In 2023, Gillian Cosgriff won the top prize at Melbourne comedy festival. (Supplied: Token/Giulia McGauran)

I aggressively recommend Tricksy Collins: Assigned Magician at Birth. I saw this show at Melbourne Fringe in 2024 and it blew my mind. Tricksy's style is so unique and such a perfect combination of smart and silly. She's absolutely brilliant and I loved every second — please imagine me kindly grabbing you by both shoulders, looking meaningfully into your eyes, and firmly saying, "You MUST book a ticket."

I also recommend Grace Jarvis for incredibly insightful stand-up, The Burton Brothers for outlandish characters, Fuccbois if you want to see a pop boy band extravaganza, Olga Koch for a magnificent international act at the top of her game, and Amy Ruffle for a brilliant improviser doing a first exciting solo show!

Gillian Cosgriff: Life, Oh Life is at Melbourne and Brisbane comedy festivals, before touring Sydney, Hobart and Geelong.

Lou Wall

Lou Wall's 2024 show won the top prize at Sydney Comedy Festival and was nominated for best show in Melbourne. (Supplied: Token/Rachel Sherlock)

If you're after some pure banger stand-up, you can't go past Annie Boyle, Henry Yan or Jess Fuchs.

For those tickled by alt comedy, Kate Dolan, Brynley Stent and Abby Wambaugh are absolute musts.

If musical comedy is your jam, Gabbi Bolt is one of the best we have and Ariana and the Rose is coming all the way from New York, which is such a treat.

I've heard phenomenal things about Angella Dravid and I cannot wait to finally catch Maybelline is in her Slut Era, which has been taking the festival circuit by storm.

If I had my way, this recommendations list would be 200 comics long, but in lieu of that, I reckon taking a punt on someone you've never heard of is the best way to experience the festival.

Lou Wall: Where Are All the Tall Grandmas? is at Melbourne, Sydney, Perth and Brisbane comedy festivals.

Sam Taunton

Sam Taunton co-hosted The Project from 2023 to 2025, alongside Sarah Harris and Waleed Aly. (Supplied: Token/Ian Laidlaw)

Every year during the comedy festival, my close friends ask: "Who should we go and see?!" I always say, "What about a guy called Sam Taunton??" They reply, "Haha, no, not you, idiot! We don't want to see you!"

Putting aside my hurt ego, I LOVE giving out recommendations and this year is no different.

Bronwyn Kuss is always one of my favourites. I've sent loads of people to her over the years and everyone walks away with sore jaws from laughing. Rowan Thambar is doing a great show about a crazy neighbour which I caught a preview of at Melbourne Fringe and absolutely loved.

Dan Rath is maybe the best comedian in this country. How he's not a household name is beyond me. Alex Ward has belted out another joke-heavy hour that might be her best work yet. Sez is so funny and really coming into her own as a live performer. Tim Batt from New Zealand is doing a show about accidentally meddling in the last Aotearoa election. He was telling me about it the other day and I was laughing so hard. I can't wait to see it.

I'm also super keen to see the character work of Hannah Camilleri, new jokes and stories from Abby Howells, plus the best and funniest comedy show of all time, Big Hewy's Footy Show.

Take a punt, see someone new, and enjoy the best four weeks of the Melbourne year.

Sam Taunton: This Must Be the Place is at Melbourne, Sydney and Brisbane comedy festivals.

Anesti Danelis

Anesti Danelis is a musical comedian from Canada, whose viral videos have copped more than 500 million views. (Supplied: Century/Dahlia Katz)

My favourite things to check out at festivals are comedians who present something unique and a little unhinged (but still hilarious obviously).

If you're looking for an absolute bonkers and entertaining show, you have to check out Marshall Lorenzo's Serving Can't. His characters and sketches are so ridiculously funny, and his joke writing is so solid. It makes sense — he's written for Canada's Drag Race, and has been nominated and won various Fringe awards. Definitely add this extravaganza to your list.

If you straight-up want to be the most surprised you've ever been in your life, you should check out Garry Starr's Classic Penguins. I saw it at Edinburgh Fringe in 2024 and nothing could have ever prepared me for this show. It's chaotic, absurd, smart, silly and hysterical. He's a seasoned pro and it's great to see how much this show has exploded since 2024. Go watch it if you want some joy in your life.

Anesti Danelis: Artificially Intelligent is at Melbourne and Sydney comedy festivals.

Elouise Eftos

Last year, Elouise Eftos became the first Australian woman to be nominated for best newcomer at Edinburgh Fringe. (Supplied: Century)

Go out and see my girls Kate Dolan, Emma Holland and Jess Fuchs — you won't be disappointed! I'm so excited to see their new shows!

Kate has energy like no one else on stage, she's alt but dark and so much fun to watch! Makes me cackle!

I wish I could live in Emma's brain, in awe of her always and her new show has me so intrigued (it's about her dog dying but it's funny, I promise!).

Jess is the best stand-up in the country and such a sharp writer! Whip-smart, so funny and her on-stage outfits are to die for!

Elouise Eftos: Aphrodite is at Melbourne, Sydney, Perth and Brisbane comedy festivals.

Harry Jun

Harry Jun is a host of ABC TV's Good Game: Spawn Squad and the podcast Say Kimchi. (Supplied: Century)

You know how you have that friend that went on a nightmare date and they retell it to you in the funniest way possible? Jenny Tian's show is like that and so much more.

He Huang will say some of the funniest/craziest things I've ever heard in my life and that's just off-stage. Her shows are even better.

David Correos's comedy is like a drum and bass track that has collided with a neutron star in the middle of a local talent show. It's unreal.

Jin Hao Li's comedy feels like an ethereal spirit is telling you jokes from an intersecting plane of existence. It feels like being haunted but in a good way, ya know what I mean?

Rowan Thambar has a knack for filling your heart and making you chuckle. I still listen to an album he made in 2020 called From Where You'd Rather Be because of this.

I like comedy with a bite and Kiyanosh Sahebi and Alan Fang are really good at this.

I love watching Ting Lim because she'll call out something that has infuriated me as well and then relieve me with a big laugh.

I love the way that Takashi Wakasugi sees the world. He'll insist it's because he's Japanese, but I think it's his own unique weirdness that makes his comedy so excellent.

Harry Jun: Inside Jokes, Outside Voice is at Melbourne, Sydney and Brisbane comedy festivals.
